reactivity_rules.v: Do not form salts. Examples: CO, NO, N₂O

blocks.text: According to the Brønsted–Lowry theory, an acid is a substance that can donate a proton (H⁺) to another particle. In aqueous solution, acids dissociate: HA → H⁺ + A⁻. The H⁺ ions (more precisely, H₃O⁺) are responsible for the sour taste, the ability to corrode metals, and indicator colour changes.

blocks.title: Strong vs. weak acids
blocks.rule: Acid strength is determined by how easily H⁺ detaches from the molecule
blocks.description:
Strong acids (HCl, HNO₃, H₂SO₄) dissociate completely: every molecule releases H⁺. Solution pH < 1.
Weak acids (H₂CO₃, H₂S, CH₃COOH) dissociate partially: intact HA molecules coexist with ions in solution. Solution pH 3–6.
The easier an acid releases H⁺, the stronger it is.

blocks.title: General acid reactions
blocks.rule: Acids react with metals, bases, basic oxides, and salts
blocks.description:
1. Acid + metal (before H₂ in the activity series) → salt + H₂↑
   Zn + 2HCl → ZnCl₂ + H₂↑
2. Acid + base → salt + water (neutralisation)
   HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O
3. Acid + basic oxide → salt + water
   2HCl + CaO → CaCl₂ + H₂O
4. Acid + salt → new acid + new salt (if gas↑, precipitate↓, or weak electrolyte forms)
   H₂SO₄ + BaCl₂ → BaSO₄↓ + 2HCl

reactivity_rules.v: Formed by dissolving nonmetal gases (HCl, H₂S, HF) in water
. Decompose on heating into oxide and water
Product of complete neutralization of an acid by a base
Product of incomplete neutralization of a polyprotic acid. Contains H in the acid residue
Product of incomplete neutralization of a polyvalent base. Contains OH⁻ groups
